item.title: This is a bit of a confusing SCP....
item.content:encoded: Staff notice: this user has been permanently banned for spamming.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image appears to be from here, with no indication it has been released under a license compliant with the wiki's (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
a subject wearing a poncho with a sleeve missing

I've yet to see a poncho with sleeves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting how they assumed the problem with the last log was that the shirt was from the 80's. I personally would have seen how screwed up it was and automatically assumed that something messed up was about to happen but maybe there is some correlation between decades that I'm just not seeing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who's wardrobe is this? That's what I want to know. What kind of person would be collecting these articles for so long? Was it a collection that became anomalous, or a collection of already similarly anomalous clothes? Does that imply thete could be other articles out there? Do clothes added to the wardrobe gain these properties because the wardrobe us doing it? Does it generate the damage on the clothing? What if damaged or brand new clothing is added? What happens?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SUBJECT: Dr. ██████ [Unplanned experiment; an unidentified individual left an article from SCP-025 on Dr. ██████'s desk that looked similar to an item of his own clothing. Any information about this incident and/or the perpetrator of same should be reported immediately to senior staff.]
ARTICLE: Lightweight scarf, dyed a number of colors
IMPERFECTION(S): Heavily pulled seam caused scarf to be considerably shorter and tighter in the middle.
TEST RESULTS: According to his itinerary, Dr. ██████, wearing the item from SCP-025, was en route to the enclosure of SCP-███ on ██/██/████ for routine testing. However, he diverged from his intended path and began in a direction towards an entirely separate wing of the facility. Subject then entered the enclosure of SCP-173 without gathering accompaniment or following safety procedures, and, upon hearing the door closing, blinked. Cause of death listed as strangulation resulting from a crushed windpipe.

Headcanon: this is the Foundation's attempt to coverup the suicide of a researcher who committed suicide by 173. There's too much details about it that feel off - some unknown individual bypassed security protocols, got a scarf that happened to look like the doctor's, snuck into his office, left the scarf on his desk, and then the doctor acted strangely to die by 173. The Foundation doesn't want people finding out about how high their suicide rate is, so they made this up as a cover story and chalked it up to an anomaly.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Shinydan, instead of replying to your own comments, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

Also, staff only delete posts that contain severe rule-breaking material, like links to virus sites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, necropost, please delete when you get the chance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was [DATA EXPUNGED] in the 80s…
Good stuff, well deserving of 200 upvotes.

item.title: (sarcastic)
item.content:encoded: So who wants to rename it the wholly fatal wardrobe? /s
Fairly good, but those last two logs killed it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, even though I find the second to last test mildly excruciating due to the unnecessary 173 crosslink and that bit of lolfoundationism. The rest of the article is above average for a 001-099.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only bit I don't like is the "weaponisation" bit, although I suppose this is due to differences in the series 1 articles to the more recent items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait a sec… in that last one experiment, is that implying the "thriller virus"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 for scientist just waltzing into 173 containment area like nothing. Some security Foundation has… *rolleyes*
Could use a little editing for tone, but this is a quality SCP that is good to have in the 000 block.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that sounds about right…or the garments might cancel each other out and do nothing at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No they didn't. SCPs create things out of nowhere all the time. Who's to say this one didn't?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would happen if a damaged article of clothing was placed in the wardrobe-would it take on the properties of the clothing already present? The originals had to get in there somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've got a backup of it.

Gimme a few minutes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture doesn't work, and seems to have been linked from a website that I believe is now defunct. Perhaps the author has an alternate picture, or someone else does?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought of that too. Presumably they'll suffer some sort of accident that warrants a scar roughly equal to the repairs done.

Or they effect would stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe that the reverse is true, and that all intact parts of the clothes should be considered invincible. I don't know how to test this though. For that matter, have we ever tried fixing one of the articles of clothing? (Without wearing it of course.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of stacking them. Let's get some Ds, and have them start packing on layers.

And what happens if the article's taken off before time of injury? Despite knowing his death was imminent, I find it strange that D-690 didn't try to remove the cap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if the flaws in the outer one cause the wearer to perform actions unwittingly that would cause the same imperfections in the inner one?
Say, if a guy's wearing a button-down shirt with a sweater over it, and the sweater's got a tear in the back, then the guy rubs up against something sharp on accident [just an example, there are easily more interesting ways this could come about]. THEN something ghastly happens through the rip in the back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if you take two garments for the same body area (like two shirts) and put them on the same D-Class so that none of their flaws overlap? My guess is death by disease, probably located according to the first article worn (but possibly developing slightly later in locations corresponding to the second).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …small lacerations on the thighs and buttocks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dare I suggest torn underwear and a healthy dose of [Data Expunged]?
